scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE District Court / Civil Court
AT Bengaluru

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT WITH COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the suit. The Defendant is a person sui juris and can be sued. This Court has jurisdiction under Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 as the contract was executed within Bengaluru, was to be performed within Bengaluru, and the Defendant resides within Bengaluru. The suit falls within the pecuniary jurisdiction of this Court as the value of the subject matter is within the prescribed limits.

COMMERCIAL COURTS ACT COMPLIANCE

2. The Plaintiff states that the suit is subject to the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 given the suit value. The Plaintiff has complied with Section 12A regarding pre-institution mediation as per {{SECTION_12A_COMPLIANCE_STATUS}}.

LIMITATION

3. The litigation is within limitation under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The filing is done well within the prescribed period.

FACTS OF THE AGREEMENT

4. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff and Defendant entered into an Agreement to Sell for the purchase of residential flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft.
5. The total consideration agreed upon was Rs. 85,00,000/-. The Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- on the date of the agreement via RTGS (UTR: HDFC20240110987654).
6. The balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was agreed to be paid on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution of the sale deed. The Agreement to Sell is marked as Annexure A.
7. The payment of advance is evidenced by the bank receipt marked as Annexure B.

PLAINTIFF'S READINESS AND WILLINGNESS

8. The Plaintiff has always been and continues to be ready and willing to perform her part of the contract.
9. The Plaintiff has arranged the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- and holds sufficient funds in her bank account to tender the same upon execution of the sale deed.
10. The Plaintiff has repeatedly offered to complete the transaction and register the conveyance as per the terms of the agreement.

DEFENDANT'S REFUSAL TO PERFORM

11. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant sent a letter refusing to convey the title, claiming he received a higher offer from a third party.
12. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to complete the transaction and perform his obligations.
13. The Defendant refused to comply with the legal notice and has failed to register the deed despite the Plaintiff's demand. The Defendant's refusal letter is marked as Annexure D and the legal notice is marked as Annexure C.

LEGAL BASIS

14. The Plaintiff is entitled to specific performance as the court shall grant enforcement under Section 10 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963.
15. The Plaintiff satisfies the condition of continuous readiness and willingness under Section 16 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 by arranging funds and offering performance.
16. The contract is valid and binding under Section 10 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872.
17. This Court has authority to try the suit under Section 9 and Section 16 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
18. The Plaintiff claims interest under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 and compensation or possession under Section 21 and Section 22 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963.

CAUSE OF ACTION

19. The cause of action first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant refused to convey the title.
20. The cause of action continues to subsist as the Defendant has failed to perform his obligations under the agreement.
21. The litigation is filed within the limitation period prescribed under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

22. For the purpose of court fee and jurisdiction, the suit is valued at {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}.
23. The requisite court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on the plaint under the applicable Court Fee Act.
24. The plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee.

PRAYER

25. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:
(a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024 directing the Defendant to convey the title in favour of the Plaintiff;
(b) Direct the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff if not already in possession;
(c) Award compensation under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963 in lieu of or in addition to specific performance, at such amount as this Hon'ble Court deems fit;
(d) Award pendente lite and future interest on the amount paid under Section 34 CPC respectively;
(e) Award costs of the suit to the Plaintiff;
(f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF SUIT PROPERTY

{{SCHEDULE_OF_SUIT_PROPERTY -- Address, Survey/Plot No., Extent/Area, Boundaries (East/West/North/South)}}

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt (UTR: HDFC20240110987654)

Annexure C — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

Annexure D — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 25 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
                                    {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
                                    Mobile: {{ADVOCATE_PHONE}}
                                    Email: {{ADVOCATE_EMAIL}}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A Company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A Limited Liability Partnership,
Having its office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT UNDER SECTION 73 OF THE INDIAN CONTRACT ACT, 1872

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The suit is valued above the specified value of {{SPECIFIED_VALUE_THRESHOLD}} as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

2. The Plaintiff is a company registered under the Companies Act and is a person sui juris competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is a limited liability partnership registered under the Limited Liability Partnership Act and is a person sui juris who can be sued in law.

3. The Defendant has its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru, and the contract was executed and was to be performed within Bengaluru. Thus, the cause of action arose wholly within the territorial limits of this Hon'ble Court. This suit is filed in accordance with Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, as the Defendant resides and carries on business within the jurisdiction of this Court.

4. The subject matter of the suit is of a commercial nature and the specified value exceeds the threshold prescribed under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try and adjudicate the present suit as the valuation is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court.

5. The Plaintiff has complied with the requirements of Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 regarding pre-institution mediation or is exempted therefrom as per the applicable provisions. The suit is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

LIMITATION

6. The suit is within limitation under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, the period prescribed being three years from the date on which the contract was broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

7. The Plaintiff confirms compliance with Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 regarding pre-institution mediation or claims exemption therefrom as applicable.

FACTS OF THE CASE

8. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a written Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system. The total contract value was agreed at Rs. 45,00,000/-. The essential terms included the delivery of the first module by 01.05.2024. A copy of the Agreement is annexed as Annexure A.

9. In pursuance of the contract, the Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000/- as the first milestone payment to the Defendant on 01.02.2024 via NEFT. The transaction bears UTR Number ICIC20240201456789. The Plaintiff has fully performed its payment obligation under the contract. A copy of the NEFT receipt is annexed as Annexure B.
The Plaintiff expected the delivery of the first module by the stipulated deadline of 01.05.2024.

BREACH OF CONTRACT

10. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module of the ERP system by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024. This constitutes a material breach of the essential terms of the Software Development Agreement.

11. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant abandoned the project without completing the deliverables and without returning the advance payment received from the Plaintiff. Email correspondence evidencing the abandonment of the project is annexed as Annexure C.

12. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 calling upon the Defendant to refund the advance and compensate for the loss. The Defendant has failed to respond to the notice or refund the amount. A copy of the legal notice is annexed as Annexure E.

LOSS AND DAMAGE

13. The Plaintiff is entitled to the refund of the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid to the Defendant, as the Defendant failed to perform any obligation under the contract. This amount represents the actual loss suffered due to non-performance.

14. The Plaintiff suffered consequential damages amounting to Rs. 12,00,000/- due to the delay in operations caused by the non-delivery of the ERP system. This loss is directly attributable to the Defendant's breach and is supported by the loss assessment report. A copy of the report is annexed as Annexure D.

15. The Plaintiff took reasonable steps to mitigate the loss by issuing the legal notice and seeking alternative remedies upon the Defendant's abandonment. The total claim for damages is Rs. 32,00,000/-, comprising the refund and consequential loss.

LEGAL BASIS

16. The contract between the parties is valid and binding under Section 37 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, as it involves lawful offer, acceptance, consideration, and competency. The parties are bound to perform their respective obligations unless dispensed with under the law.

17. Time was of the essence in the contract regarding the delivery date of 01.05.2024. The Defendant's failure to perform by the stipulated date invokes Section 55 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, rendering the contract voidable at the option of the Plaintiff.

18. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation for any loss or damage caused by the breach under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. The damages claimed are a genuine pre-estimate of loss and not penal in nature.

19. This Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to entertain the suit under Section 9 and Section 20 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. The Plaintiff is also entitled to interest on the decree amount under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

20. There has been no novation, accord, or satisfaction of the original contract under Section 62-63 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872. The Plaintiff has mitigated loss to the extent possible, and no defence of failure to mitigate is available against the Plaintiff.

CAUSE OF ACTION

21. The cause of action first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and failed to deliver the module.

22. The suit is filed within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Limitation Act, 1963, as the breach occurred within three years from the date of filing the suit.

23. The right to sue arose from the specific omission by the Defendant to deliver the software module and refund the advance payment as agreed in the contract.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

24. The total value of the suit for the purpose of jurisdiction and court fee is Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only). This valuation covers the claim for refund and consequential damages.

25. The requisite court fee of {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} has been paid on the plaint. The plaint is properly stamped and tendered with the requisite court fee as per the Karnataka Court Fees Act.

26. The valuation is made for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee, and the Plaintiff submits that the suit is properly valued within the pecuniary limits of this Hon'ble Court.

PRAYER

27. In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

(a) Award damages in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for Rs. 32,00,000/- towards refund of advance and consequential loss;
(b) Award interest at 18% per annum on the said amount from the date of breach till realisation;
(c) Award future interest at such rate as th
